references.title: Do Not Trust a Model Because It is Confident: Uncovering and Characterizing Unknown Unknowns to Student Success Predictors in Online-Based Learning

extracted.Students: 292 Algebra, 216 Functional Programming and 147 Parallelism and Concurrency Students
extracted.Courses: 3 Courses of a Computer Science and Communication Systems BSc degree
extracted.Features: [22 Control Variables, 13 Effort variables, 7 Proactivity variables, 3 Regularity variables]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Indicators were extracted per week. Normalization was then applied. 

Nested CV was used and models are course-specific
extracted.Comments:
Authors use a multi-tiered system to identify what information may be missing from student performance prediction models. 

Essentially, the prediction modules output a probability of passing or failing. These will sub-sequently be used as inputs for a clustering model to map differences in model perception vs reality (identify potential unknown unknowns.)
extracted.Limitations:
Course-Sepcifc Models. 

Small number of algorithms tested.

extracted.Features: [22 Control Variables, 13 Effort variables, 7 Proactivity variables, 3 Regularity variables]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Indicators were extracted per week. Normalization was then applied. 

Nested CV was used and models are course-specific
extracted.Comments:
Authors use a multi-tiered system to identify what information may be missing from student performance prediction models. 

Essentially, the prediction modules output a probability of passing or failing. These will sub-sequently be used as inputs for a clustering model to map differences in model perception vs reality (identify potential unknown unknowns.)

Performance on MOOCs is much better than performance of Flipped Classroom settings.
extracted.Limitations:
Course-Sepcifc Models. 

Small number of algorithms tested.

extracted.Students: 292 Algebra, 216 Functional Programming and 147 Parallelism and Concurrency Students,  15394 DSP,  2423 HWTS amnd 18702 GP students
extracted.Data sources: Student Performance Model Predictions, LMS logs, Exam Grade
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Authors created a nomenclature for different types of students based on the difference between confidence in prediction and actual label: 

Known knowns: Model is very confident and prediction matches label.

Known unknowns: Low condfidence prediction.

Unknown Unknowns: High confidence prediction of incorrect label. 

Based on this observation, the authors create different prediction models and try to characterize the different groups.
extracted.Cluster Description:
Flipped Classroom models have a higher percentage of Unknowns that MOOC models. With RF being better at identifying them.

However, MMOOCs also exhibit the existence of Unknowns. 

When characterizing Unknowns Unknowns in a Flipped Classroom setting… the authors identify multiple variables associated with frequency and engagement as relevant for the prediction.
extracted.Implications:
Scientific: 

Data is partial in nature and does not capture all student behavior. So, having a model only assess performance based on accuracy may lead to misleading results.

Techniological: 

Need for better ways of identifying unknown unknowns since the current models struggle with them.

Social:

Need to introduce interpretability for trust in implementation.
extracted.Comments: Authors create multiple classifiers for the types of mistakes of their models.
extracted.Limitations:
Course-Sepcifc Models. 

Small number of algorithms tested.

Flipped Classroom Lessons data not used.

annotations.text: Insufficient representation is a common cause of weak spots in predictive models. The paper intends to provide explanatory potential for areas where a predictive model may fail - called unknown unknowns (situations where a model is very confident but gets the prediction wrong).

Thus, the paper starts with the introduction of a framework that intends to identify what are the unknown unknowns - reasons for why a student failed and that the model was blind to. The mdeol is verified using data from flipped and online courses. It also combines quantitative analysis with instructor interviews.
